input.prompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.content:

Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix

input.initialPrompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.reasoningText:
The user migrated from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform (MTP) and is hitting exit code 8 when test assemblies produce zero tests. This is a known behavioral difference between VSTest and MTP — MTP returns a non-zero exit code (8) when no tests are discovered, whereas VSTest treated that as success.

Let me look at their project files to understand their setup, then provide the fix.

data.content:
This is a known MTP behavioral difference. **Exit code 8 means "no tests discovered"** — MTP treats that as an error, whereas VSTest silently returned 0.

Since your tests are conditionally compiled out (`#if INCLUDE_SLOW_TESTS`), MTP finds zero tests and fails the process.

**Fix:** Tell MTP to ignore exit code 8 by setting the `TESTINGPLATFORM_EXITCODE_IGNORE` environment variable in your CI pipeline:

```yaml
# In your CI pipeline (e.g., Azure DevOps, GitHub Actions):
env:
  TESTINGPLATFORM_EXITCODE_IGNORE: 8
```

Or, if you want to apply it at the project level so it's always in effect, add this MSBuild property to your `.csproj` (or a `Directory.Build.props`):

```xml
<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
```

Let me apply the project-level fix:

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- MTP returns exit code 8 when zero tests are discovered (unlike VSTest which returned 0).
         Ignore it so conditional-compilation configurations that produce no tests don't fail CI. -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- MTP returns exit code 8 when zero tests are discovered (unlike VSTest which returned 0).
         Ignore it so conditional-compilation configurations that produce no tests don't fail CI. -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.content:
Verified — `dotnet test` now exits with **code 0** even with zero tests discovered.

**Summary of the fix:**

| | VSTest | MTP (default) | MTP (with fix) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 tests found | exit 0 ✅ | exit 8 ❌ | exit 0 ✅ |

The `--ignore-exit-code 8` flag tells MTP to treat "no tests discovered" as success, matching the old VSTest behavior. You can alternatively set the environment variable `TESTINGPLATFORM_EXITCODE_IGNORE=8` in your CI pipeline if you prefer not to bake it into the project file.
